description: Fundamental GPU-parallel algorithmic patterns: reduction, prefix scan, histogram, sorting, and the work-efficiency vs step-efficiency tradeoff that governs all parallel algorithm design.

concepts.title: Parallel Reduction
concepts.preciseDefinition: An algorithm that reduces N elements to a single value using a binary associative operation (sum, max, min) in O(log N) steps by having threads pair up and combine, halving active threads each step. A naïve GPU reduction with N threads has step complexity O(log N) but work complexity O(N log N) — work-inefficient. The optimized version (Mark Harris / NVIDIA) achieves O(N) work with careful warp shuffle and shared memory patterns.
keyFacts: Step 1 (naïve): threads reduce pairs in shared memory with stride; has warp divergence and bank conflicts
Step 2: reversed indexing eliminates divergence (lower-indexed threads always active)
Step 3: sequential addressing with stride halving — coalesced shared memory access
Step 4: unroll last warp (warp-synchronous execution, no __syncthreads needed for 32 threads)
Step 5: warp-level primitives __shfl_down_sync — avoid shared memory entirely for final warp
Step 6: thread coarsening — each thread first reduces multiple elements before warp reduction
commonMisconceptions: The simplest reduction tree is work-efficient — stride-1 reduction with active high-indexed threads causes divergence
Atomic operations can replace reduction kernels — atomicAdd is slower than tree reduction for large N
__syncthreads() is needed inside warp reduction — threads in the same warp execute synchronously (pre-Volta)

concepts.title: Warp Shuffle Instructions
concepts.preciseDefinition: Hardware instructions (__shfl_sync, __shfl_down_sync, __shfl_xor_sync, __shfl_up_sync) that allow threads within the same warp to directly read each other's registers without shared memory. Available on cc3.0+. A warp-level reduction using __shfl_down_sync requires 5 instructions (log2(32)=5 steps) and zero shared memory for the final warp.
keyFacts: __shfl_down_sync(0xffffffff, val, offset): thread i receives val from thread i+offset
__shfl_xor_sync(0xffffffff, val, mask): butterfly exchange for prefix operations
mask parameter: bitmask of participating threads (use 0xffffffff for all 32)
Width parameter limits exchange to sub-warp groups (e.g., width=8 for 8-thread groups)
Warp reduction pattern: for (int offset=16; offset>0; offset>>=1) val += __shfl_down_sync(0xffffffff, val, offset);
commonMisconceptions: Shuffle works across different warps — shuffles are strictly intra-warp
Older __shfl (without _sync) is safe — deprecated; use _sync variants with explicit mask to avoid undefined behavior on Volta+
Shuffle requires shared memory — shuffles use the warp's own register file, no shared memory

exercises.title: Parallel Reduction Sum
exercises.whatItTeaches: Iterative optimization of a reduction: naïve → divergence-free → __shfl_down_sync → thread coarsening. Understanding why each step improves performance. Two-pass reduction for large arrays.
exercises.starterCodeHint: // Warp reduction: for(int s=warpSize/2; s>0; s>>=1) val+=__shfl_down_sync(0xffffffff,val,s); // Then store to smem[warpLane] and do block-level reduction
exercises.testCaseHint: Test N=1 to N=128M; compare result vs CPU reference sum; measure vs cuBLAS cublasSasum

exercises.title: Parallel Max Reduction
exercises.whatItTeaches: Applying the reduction pattern to non-commutative-but-associative operations (max); atomic max as alternative; how to template reduction kernels for different operators.
exercises.testCaseHint: Compare against std::max_element on CPU; test with all-equal, monotone, and random arrays

concepts.title: Parallel Prefix Scan
concepts.preciseDefinition: An algorithm that computes the running prefix sum (or other binary associative operation) of N elements. Inclusive scan: output[i] = input[0] + ... + input[i]. Exclusive scan: output[i] = input[0] + ... + input[i-1]. The Blelloch algorithm achieves O(N) work and O(log N) steps using an upsweep (reduce) phase followed by a downsweep (broadcast) phase.
keyFacts: Blelloch scan: upsweep (reduce tree) + downsweep (distribute) — O(2N) work, O(2 log N) steps
Naïve Hillis-Steele scan: O(N log N) work, O(log N) steps — not work-efficient
Large arrays: single-block scan + inter-block scan + add offsets (3-pass pattern)
Thrust library: thrust::inclusive_scan / exclusive_scan — use for production code
Applications: stream compaction, radix sort, histogram equalization, variable-length data indexing
commonMisconceptions: Prefix scan is equivalent to reduction — reduction collapses to one value; scan preserves all prefix results
Hillis-Steele is always better because it has fewer steps — it has O(N log N) work which hurts large N
CUDA has a built-in prefix scan — must use Thrust or CUB; no single CUDA intrinsic for block scan

concepts.title: Work Efficiency vs Step Efficiency
concepts.preciseDefinition: Work complexity is the total number of operations across all processors; step complexity (depth) is the number of parallel rounds. A work-efficient algorithm performs O(N) total operations (same as the sequential algorithm); a step-efficient algorithm minimizes the number of rounds. The ideal parallel algorithm is both: work-efficient AND step-efficient. GPU performance depends on both because work-inefficient algorithms waste CUDA Core throughput.
keyFacts: Naïve reduction tree: O(N log N) work (work-inefficient), O(log N) steps
Optimal parallel reduction: O(N) work, O(log N) steps — achieved with per-thread sequential partial reduction + tree
Hillis-Steele scan: O(N log N) work, O(log N) steps — step-efficient but work-inefficient
Blelloch scan: O(N) work, O(2 log N) steps — work-efficient
On GPU with P processors: optimal if N/P ≥ log N (sufficient parallelism for work-efficient algorithm)
commonMisconceptions: The fastest parallel algorithm always has the fewest steps — work-inefficient algorithms can be slower due to wasted compute
Work efficiency only matters for CPUs — on GPUs with limited arithmetic bandwidth, work-inefficient algorithms saturate the compute units unnecessarily

exercises.title: Exclusive Prefix Scan
exercises.whatItTeaches: Blelloch upsweep/downsweep algorithm; two-level (block + grid) scan for large arrays; why the naive approach is work-inefficient; using warp shuffles for the final warp.
exercises.starterCodeHint: // Upsweep: for(int stride=1; stride<blockDim.x; stride<<=1) { if (tid%(2*stride)==2*stride-1) smem[tid]+=smem[tid-stride]; } // Then downsweep
exercises.testCaseHint: Verify exclusive_scan(input)[i] == sum(input[0..i-1]) for all i; test N=1024, 65536, 10M

concepts.title: Parallel Histogram
concepts.preciseDefinition: Computing a frequency distribution of N elements into B bins on a GPU. Challenge: atomic update contention when multiple threads write to the same bin simultaneously. Solution: privatization — each block maintains a shared memory histogram; blocks then atomically merge partial histograms into global memory.
keyFacts: Naïve: atomicAdd on global memory — high contention for small B (e.g., 256 bins, thousands of threads hit same bin)
Privatization: shared memory per-block histogram → bank conflicts possible if bin count < 32
Inter-block merge: atomicAdd on global with per-block partial results
Warp aggregation: coalesce atomic operations from threads in same warp hitting same bin — reduces atomic pressure by up to 32×
CUB DeviceHistogram for production use — handles all strategies automatically
commonMisconceptions: Atomic operations are always slow — atomics to L2 cache (Ampere+) are much faster than global atomics
More bins always mean fewer conflicts — with 256 bins and 65536 threads, expected 256 threads per bin on average

exercises.title: Privatized Histogram
exercises.whatItTeaches: Atomic operations in shared and global memory; privatization pattern; contention analysis; comparison of naïve vs privatized vs warp-aggregated approaches.
exercises.starterCodeHint: __shared__ unsigned int smem_hist[NUM_BINS]; atomicAdd(&smem_hist[bin], 1); __syncthreads(); atomicAdd(&global_hist[tid_in_block], smem_hist[tid_in_block]);
exercises.testCaseHint: Verify histogram correctness vs CPU reference; benchmark N=10M with B=64,256,1024 bins

concepts.title: Radix Sort on GPU
concepts.preciseDefinition: A non-comparison-based sort that processes integer keys digit-by-digit (radix), performing one stable counting sort pass per digit. GPU radix sort uses: (1) per-block digit histogram, (2) exclusive prefix scan over histograms, (3) scatter to output. With 4-bit digits (base-16), 8 passes sort 32-bit integers. Step complexity O(k) where k=num_digits; work complexity O(kN).
keyFacts: CUB DeviceRadixSort and Thrust sort_by_key are the standard production implementations
GPU radix sort: ~3-5 GB/s throughput for 32-bit integers on modern GPUs
Digit size tradeoff: larger digit (8-bit) = fewer passes but larger histogram (256 bins) and more shared memory
NVIDIA's best radix sort achieves ~95% of HBM bandwidth on H100
Sorting networks (bitonic, odd-even merge) are used for small arrays (<= 1M elements)
commonMisconceptions: GPU sorting is always faster than CPU sorting — for small N (<10K), CPU sort is faster due to launch overhead
Radix sort handles all data types — only works natively on fixed-width unsigned integers; float requires bit manipulation

concepts.title: Map, Scatter, Gather
concepts.preciseDefinition: Three fundamental data-parallel primitives. Map: apply a function f to every element independently — trivially parallel, embarrassingly so. Scatter: each thread writes to a computed destination index — contention risk when multiple threads scatter to same address. Gather: each thread reads from a computed source index — non-coalesced access pattern, often cache-friendly if indices are clustered.
keyFacts: Map is the basis of vectorAdd, SAXPY, and most element-wise neural network operations
Gather with random indices: memory access is random — use texture memory for read-only gather with spatial locality
Scatter with conflicts requires atomic operations or privatization
Compaction (stream compaction) = gather of elements satisfying a predicate: uses prefix scan to compute output indices
commonMisconceptions: Scatter and gather are symmetric in performance — scatter can cause write conflicts; gather cannot
Map always achieves peak memory bandwidth — only if accesses are coalesced

coverageGaps: Merge sort GPU implementation — classic parallel algorithm, good for teaching parallel pattern composition
Stream compaction exercise — important building block for sparse operations, not yet planned as standalone exercise
Atomic operations deep dive — hardware atomics, CAS loops, lock-free data structures — referenced but no exercise
